learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Loops, Sliding Window, Basic Mathematics, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine tracking the average temperature over every consecutive k days. Instead of recalculating each average from scratch, slide the window by one day and update the sum efficiently.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine tracking the average temperature over every consecutive k days. Instead of recalculating each average from scratch, slide the window by one day and update the sum efficiently.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use the Fixed Size Sliding Window pattern whenever the problem asks about a contiguous subarray of fixed length with maximum or minimum sum, average, or count.
learning.signals: Whenever a problem asks for a contiguous subarray of fixed size with maximum/minimum sum, average, or count, think of the Fixed Size Sliding Window pattern.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: You are given an integer array nums consisting of n elements, and an integer k. Find the contiguous subarray of length k that has the maximum average value and return this value. Any answer with a calculation error less than 10⁻⁵ will be accepted.

examples.explanation: The subarray [12,-5,-6,50] has sum 51 and average 12.75.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Calculate the sum of every window of size k independently and compute its average.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Since the window size is fixed, maintain the sum of the current window. As the window moves, subtract the outgoing element and add the incoming element. Keep track of the maximum window sum.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: A fixed-size window can be updated in O(1) while sliding.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever a problem asks for a contiguous subarray of fixed size with maximum/minimum sum, average, or count, think of the Fixed Size Sliding Window pattern.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Each element enters and leaves the window exactly once, resulting in a linear-time solution with constant extra space.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever a problem asks for a contiguous subarray of fixed size with maximum/minimum sum, average, or count, think of the Fixed Size Sliding Window pattern.
commonWrongPaths: Recomputing the entire window every time.
Forgetting to subtract the outgoing element.
Returning maximum sum instead of average.

hints: Slide the window one element at a time.
commonMisconceptions: Recomputing the entire window every time.
Forgetting to subtract the outgoing element.
Returning maximum sum instead of average.

pattern.name: Fixed Size Sliding Window
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever a problem asks for a contiguous subarray of fixed size with maximum/minimum sum, average, or count, think of the Fixed Size Sliding Window pattern.

approaches.title: Compute Every Window
approaches.idea: Calculate the sum of every window of size k independently and compute its average.
algorithm: Generate every window of size k.

code.java: Nested loop solution omitted because of quadratic complexity.

approaches.title: Prefix Sum
approaches.idea: Build a prefix sum array so every window sum can be calculated in constant time.
algorithm: Compute every window sum using prefix sums.

approaches.title: Fixed Size Sliding Window
approaches.idea: Compute the sum of the first window. Slide the window by removing the leftmost element and adding the next element. Update the maximum window sum after every move and finally divide by k.
approaches.intuition: Each element enters and leaves the window exactly once, resulting in a linear-time solution with constant extra space.
algorithm: Initialize maxSum with the first window.
Slide the window one position at a time.
approaches.dryRun:
nums=[1,12,-5,-6,50,3], k=4
First Sum=2
Next Sum=51
Max=51
Next Sum=42
Answer=51/4=12.75

code.python:
def findMaxAverage(nums, k):
    windowSum = sum(nums[:k])
    maxSum = windowSum

    for i in range(k, len(nums)):
        windowSum += nums[i] - nums[i-k]
        maxSum = max(maxSum, windowSum)

    return maxSum / k

visualization.description: Move a fixed-size window across the array.

interviewFollowUps: Why is Sliding Window better than brute force?
What if the window size changes?
How would you find the minimum average?
How would you solve Maximum Average Subarray II?
interviewTips: Explain why recalculating each window is inefficient.
Track the window sum instead of the average.
State O(n) time and O(1) space.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: A fixed-size window can be updated in O(1) while sliding.
cheatSheet.algorithm: First Window → Slide → Update Sum → Track Maximum → Divide by k.

importantPoints: Each element enters and leaves once.
commonMistakes: Recomputing the entire window every time.
Forgetting to subtract the outgoing element.
Returning maximum sum instead of average.
